accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Confirm CKD, stage risk, and identify red flags.

  • Confirm persistence for at least 3 months (repeat eGFR/ACR as required).
  • Stage using both G-stage (eGFR) and A-stage (ACR).
  • Check baseline bloods and urine, blood pressure, cardiovascular risk, and medication risks.
  • In CKD G3a-G5, calculate KFRE 5-year risk to support referral decisions.
  • Escalate urgently if rapid decline, nephritic/vasculitic picture, severe hyperkalaemia, or acute-on-chronic instability.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
Most stable CKD should be managed proactively in primary care.

  • Optimise blood pressure and RAAS blockade where indicated.
  • Use SGLT2 inhibitor treatment when eligible under current guidance.
  • Treat cardiovascular risk (including statin therapy unless contraindicated).
  • Optimise diabetes care and support smoking cessation.
  • Avoid nephrotoxins and adjust medicine doses to renal function.

Key thresholds:

  • ACR ≥70 mg/mmol: refer.
  • KFRE 5-year risk >5% (CKD G3a-G5): refer.
  • Persistent progression despite optimisation: refer.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Hospital admission (same day)

  • Suspected rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis/vasculitis with systemic features.
  • Severe hyperkalaemia or acute deterioration needing immediate specialist care.
  • Acute-on-chronic kidney deterioration with clinical instability.

Urgent suspicion of cancer (USOC)

  • Haematuria meeting urology cancer pathway criteria (use local USOC route).

Urgent (non-cancer outpatient)

  • Rapid sustained decline in renal function.
  • Heavy proteinuria with concerning progression.
  • Suspected multisystem renal disease.

Routine (outpatient)

  • KFRE 5-year risk >5% (CKD G3a-G5).
  • ACR ≥70 mg/mmol.
  • Progressive CKD despite optimal treatment.
  • Resistant hypertension with CKD or diagnostic uncertainty.

Include in referral

  • eGFR and ACR trend with dates.
  • BP trend and current treatment.
  • Relevant blood/urine results and imaging.
  • Comorbidities and current medicines.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html: Do not routinely refer stable, low-risk CKD with controlled blood pressure and no high-risk progression features. Continue structured primary care monitoring.

accordion_sections.heading: References
accordion_sections.html:
1. NICE NG203: Chronic kidney disease.
  2. NICE NG136: Hypertension in adults.
  3. Right Decisions NHS D&G: Chronic Kidney Disease.
